title: What Are Blue Light Glasses? How They Work and Who Needs Them
description: Blue light glasses filter high-energy visible light from screens and LED sources. Learn how they work, what the research says, and whether you should get a pair.
tldr: Blue light glasses have lenses that filter a portion of high-energy visible (HEV) blue light emitted by screens, LED bulbs, and sunlight. Research on eye strain benefits is mixed, but many wearers report improved comfort during long screen sessions. They cost $20-$100 as an add-on to prescription lenses.

definition.text: Blue light glasses are eyewear with lenses designed to filter or block a portion of blue light (wavelengths around 415–455 nm) emitted by digital screens, LED lighting, and the sun. They're available as non-prescription or prescription lenses.

sections.content:
Blue light is part of the visible light spectrum, sitting between violet and green with wavelengths of roughly 380–500 nanometres. It's the highest-energy visible light your eyes encounter daily.

Blue light glasses use one of two methods to reduce your blue light exposure:

Coating-Based Filtering

A special coating on the lens surface reflects a percentage of blue light away from your eyes. These lenses look mostly clear but may have a subtle blue or yellow tint. The coating is applied on top of the lens, similar to an anti-reflective coating.

Lens Material Filtering

The blue light filter is built directly into the lens material during manufacturing. This approach tends to filter more consistently across the lens and doesn't wear off over time like coatings can. These lenses often have a faint amber or yellow tone.

Neither method blocks 100% of blue light — and you wouldn't want that, since some blue light exposure is healthy and necessary for regulating your sleep-wake cycle.

Here's where things get nuanced. The evidence on blue light glasses is legitimately mixed.

What research supports:

  • Blue light exposure in the evening can suppress melatonin production and disrupt sleep patterns. Filtering blue light before bed may improve sleep quality.
  • High-energy blue light can cause photochemical damage to retinal cells in laboratory conditions, though the doses used in studies far exceed normal screen exposure.

What research doesn't fully support:

  • The Canadian Association of Optometrists notes that digital eye strain (tired eyes, headaches, dry eyes from screens) is primarily caused by reduced blinking and sustained close focus — not specifically by blue light.
  • A 2023 Cochrane review of 17 randomized controlled trials found no strong evidence that blue light filtering lenses reduce eye strain symptoms compared to standard lenses.

The practical reality: Many people who wear blue light glasses report feeling more comfortable during long screen sessions. Whether this is the filtering, a placebo effect, or simply a reminder to blink and take breaks doesn't change the fact that they feel better wearing them.

sections.h2: Who Benefits Most from Blue Light Glasses
sections.content:
Even with mixed research, certain groups tend to get the most value from blue light filtering:

  • People who use screens for 6+ hours daily — office workers, developers, gamers, students. Even if the benefit is partly behavioural, wearing designated "screen glasses" creates a mental cue to be mindful of screen habits.
  • Anyone who uses screens within 2 hours of bedtime — this is where the melatonin-suppression research is strongest. Filtering blue light in the evening can genuinely help with falling asleep.
  • People with light sensitivity — some individuals are more sensitive to high-energy light, and filtering it provides noticeable comfort.
  • Kids and teenagers — children's eyes transmit more blue light to the retina than adult eyes. The long-term effects of heavy screen exposure in young eyes are still being studied, so precaution makes sense.

If you spend your workday in front of a computer in Edmonton, Charm Optical offers blue light lenses as an add-on to any prescription — or as standalone non-prescription computer glasses.

sections.h2: Blue Light Glasses vs. Other Solutions
sections.content:
SolutionFilters Blue LightReduces Eye StrainCostConvenience
Blue light glassesYes (partial)Possibly$20–$100 add-onWear and forget
Night mode / f.luxYes (screen-only)PossiblyFreeSoftware setting
20-20-20 ruleNoYes (proven)FreeRequires discipline
Anti-reflective coatingMinimalYes (reduces glare)$30–$80Built into lenses
Monitor brightness adjustmentNoYesFreeOne-time setup

The most effective approach combines multiple solutions. Blue light glasses plus the 20-20-20 rule (every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds) addresses both the light exposure and the sustained-focus component of screen fatigue.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

sections.h2: How to Choose Blue Light Glasses
sections.content:
If you decide blue light glasses are worth trying, here's what to look for:

Prescription vs. Non-Prescription

If you already wear glasses, adding a blue light filter to your prescription lenses makes the most sense — one pair handles everything. If you have perfect vision, non-prescription blue light glasses are available as computer-specific eyewear.

Filter Strength

Lenses that filter 20-40% of blue light are suitable for everyday use without significantly altering colour perception. Lenses that filter 50%+ have a noticeable yellow tint and are better reserved for evening screen use.

Coating vs. In-Lens

In-lens filtering (where the blue light absorption is built into the lens material) is generally more durable than surface coatings. Ask your optician which type they use.

The team at Charm Optical can walk you through the blue light lens options and help you decide whether a filter makes sense for your screen habits and prescription.

Screens get all the attention, but they're not actually the biggest source of blue light in your life. Sunlight contains far more blue light than any phone, tablet, or monitor. LED and fluorescent lighting also emits significant blue light.

This is worth knowing because it puts screen-related blue light in context. Your 8-hour workday in front of a computer exposes you to a fraction of the blue light you'd get from a 30-minute walk outside.

For outdoor blue light, standard UV-protective sunglasses handle the job. Brands like Oakley and Maui Jim include blue light filtering as part of their lens technology.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter — no upfront costs and no claim forms to submit afterwards.

Whether you're buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the entire process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Do blue light glasses actually work?
faqs.a: They do filter blue light — that's measurable. Whether that filtering reduces eye strain is debated. Research on sleep improvement is stronger. Many people find them comfortable for long screen sessions regardless of clinical evidence. Charm Optical can demonstrate the difference with and without blue light lenses.

faqs.q: Can I add blue light filtering to my existing prescription?
faqs.a: Yes. Blue light filtering can be added to virtually any prescription lens as either a coating or built into the lens material. It typically adds $20–$100 to the lens cost. Ask your optician at Charm Optical about options.

faqs.q: Do blue light glasses have a yellow tint?
faqs.a: Mild filters (20-40%) are nearly clear with perhaps a very faint yellow hue. Stronger filters (50%+) have a noticeable amber tint. For everyday wear, most people prefer the nearly-clear version.

faqs.q: Should kids wear blue light glasses?
faqs.a: Children's eyes let in more blue light than adults' eyes. If your child spends significant time on screens, blue light glasses are a reasonable precaution. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends regular eye exams for children — book one at Charm Optical to discuss screen habits with an optometrist.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
